title: The Story of Saint Giong
synopsis:
Long ago, when Emperor Hung Vuong the Sixth ruled Vietnam, the country was peaceful and rich. The Red River was full of boats, and the green rice fields went far into the distance. People were happy and worked hard.
One day, enemies from the North came to attack. They burned towns, killed many people, and made everyone afraid. The emperor’s soldiers could not stop them. The emperor sent messengers to look for someone strong enough to save the country, but no one could help.
In a small village called Phu Dong, there lived an old couple with their little boy. The boy was already three years old, but he could not walk or talk. His parents were very sad.
One day, a messenger came to the village. He shouted the emperor’s message, asking for a hero to fight the enemies. Suddenly, the boy sat up and said to his parents, “Please call the messenger. I want to help our country.” The parents were very surprised, but they called the messenger.
The boy said to the messenger, “Go and tell the emperor I need an iron horse, iron armor, and an iron rod.” The messenger quickly went back to the capital and told the emperor. The emperor ordered blacksmiths to make the iron horse, armor, and rod.
While waiting, the boy ate a lot of food every day. He ate so much that he grew bigger and taller until he became a giant.
When the iron horse and armor arrived, the boy stood up, put on the armor, and took the iron rod. He sat on the iron horse, which made loud sounds and breathed fire. The young men of the village followed him to fight.
Giong rode his horse to the battlefield. The fire from the horse burned many enemies. Giong used his iron rod to hit them again and again. The enemies ran away, leaving many dead and wounded.
More enemies came, but Giong fought them bravely. His iron rod broke, so he pulled out bamboo trees and used them to fight. Finally, he killed the last enemy general. The war was over, and the country was safe again.
Giong told his soldiers to return to the emperor with the good news. Then he rode his iron horse up Soc Son Mountain, took off his armor, and flew to heaven. People believed he became a god.
The emperor built a temple to remember Giong and gave him the title “Heavenly King of Phu Dong.” Every year, people celebrate the Saint Giong Festival to honor the brave hero who saved the country.
